video_title: Fourth Turning To Turn The Dollar Into A Wrecking Ball? | George Gammon
gist: George Gammon argues a rising dollar is quietly strangling US trading partners via a self-reinforcing 'dollar death spiral' (dollars as both asset and liability), while private credit stress, retiring boomers unwinding the passive equity bid, and MicroStrategy's forced Bitcoin selling all signal a late-stage credit cycle; New Harbor Financial's advisors largely agree on the risks but hold a more diversified, hedged portfolio (gold/silver, non-US equities, sector rotation) rather than Gammon's pure dollar-strength call.

insights.claim: George Gammon believes political polarization is intensifying in a repeating cycle (left wins, overcorrects, right wins, overcorrects) globally, including in the US and Latin America, because underlying economic issues are never actually resolved by either party.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Expect continued whipsaw policy risk (regulatory, fiscal, trade) across election cycles rather than durable centrist stability.

insights.claim: Adam Taggart frames the US K-shaped economy — AI capex propping up headline growth while young people struggle with housing affordability and family formation — as the fuel behind rising support for extreme political candidates like Zohran Mamdani-style socialism.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Political risk to capital markets (wealth taxes, price controls, anti-business policy) rises as long as headline growth masks bottom-of-the-K distress.

insights.claim: Gammon's policy heuristic is to judge any government action by whether it increases or decreases free-market capitalism; by that standard, government equity stakes in companies like Nvidia or rare-earth firms, and a potential OpenAI bailout, count as decreasing free-market capitalism regardless of which party does it.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Watch for creeping state-directed capital allocation (equity stakes, bailouts) in AI/critical-minerals names as a distortion of normal risk pricing, not a bullish subsidy.
insights.quote: that's not increasing free market capitalism by definition that is decreasing free market capitalism

insights.claim: Gammon argues that repeatedly bailing out failing companies (banks, private credit funds, potentially OpenAI) weakens the overall economic system over time, analogous to preventing natural selection in a lion pride, because it lets the weakest 'breed' rather than fail and free up resources for stronger competitors.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A no-one-fails policy regime should be read as a long-run bearish signal for systemic resilience, even if it suppresses near-term volatility.

insights.claim: Colombia's 2025-26 presidential election saw a pro-business, US-friendly, first-time political candidate (backed implicitly by market expectations) defeat a further-left socialist successor to the outgoing president, with the Colombian peso appreciating on Polymarket odds as his chances rose.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A tradeable pattern: EM currencies can be a leading indicator of pro-business election outcomes before the vote itself.

insights.claim: Gammon argues the DXY index is misleading because it's 57% euro and 14% yen; the real story investors are missing is the much larger depreciation of Asian/EM currencies (yen, Indian rupee, Indonesian rupiah, South Korean won) against the dollar over the past 1-5 years.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Track USD/JPY, USD/INR, USD/IDR and USD/KRW cross-rates directly rather than DXY to gauge true dollar-strength stress on trading partners.

insights.claim: Because countries like Japan must source dollars both to pay for dollar-denominated commodity imports (oil) and to defend their own currency, a weakening yen makes the same barrel of oil proportionally more expensive, forcing government fuel subsidies that pump more yen into FX markets and further weaken the currency — a reinforcing spiral.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Energy-importing, currency-weak EM economies face compounding inflation risk beyond what oil price alone suggests; watch for fuel-subsidy fiscal strain as an early warning.

insights.claim: Japan's Ministry of Finance/BOJ has repeatedly intervened by selling dollar FX reserves to defend the yen whenever it approaches 160 (it is at 162 as of this recording), producing only temporary yen appreciation before it drifts back to the defense line — a pattern Gammon says only works until reserves run low.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A break of repeated BOJ defense lines without recovery would signal FX-reserve exhaustion and potential forced yen-asset selling — a major macro risk event to watch for.

insights.claim: Gammon's key framework: dollars created via bank lending are simultaneously an asset (for the recipient) and a liability (for the borrower), meaning nearly every dollar in existence carries embedded future demand as debtors must find dollars to service that debt — this constant 'bid' explains dollar strength independent of US fiscal deficits.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Don't expect deficits alone to weaken the dollar; the debt-servicing bid from dollar-denominated global borrowing is a structural offsetting force.
insights.quote: dollars aren't just an asset on a balance sheet. Dollars are simultaneously a liability on a balance sheet.

insights.claim: Gammon predicts that within the next five years, sustained dollar strength destroying US trading partners' purchasing power will force a coordinated intervention resembling the 1985 Plaza Accord, or at minimum a major expansion of central bank swap lines (which he notes are still debt that must be repaid with interest, not free liquidity).
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A future coordinated dollar-weakening intervention would be a major regime-change catalyst for EM equities, gold, and USD-short trades — worth pre-positioning research for.
insights.quote: I'd be surprised in the next five years if we didn't get a Plaza Accord 2.0.

insights.claim: Gammon argues the dollar structurally cannot fall as far as some expect (historically bottoming near DXY 96-97 rather than the 80-85 level some anticipate) because the mechanism by which dollars are lent into existence creates a persistent underlying bid.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Sets a rough structural floor for dollar-bear positioning; a break meaningfully below ~96-97 DXY would itself be a notable signal against this thesis.

insights.claim: Mike Preston estimates the offshore 'Eurodollar' system of dollar-denominated debt outside the US at roughly $13-20 trillion, which he argues explains why a full dollar collapse is unlikely: there is no comparable venue for that much dollar liquidity to be redeployed into.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Frames dollar-collapse tail-risk trades as lower probability than dollar-headwind (gradual decline) trades over a multi-decade horizon.

insights.claim: New Harbor's house view diverges from Gammon's: they expect the dollar to face structural headwinds over the next 10-20 years (versus Gammon's case for renewed medium-term dollar strength), aligning more with Brent Johnson's 'dollar milkshake theory' only for the early/crisis phase of dollar strength.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Investors following Gammon's dollar-strength thesis should note informed practitioners expect it to be a shorter-cycle phenomenon within a longer-term dollar downtrend, affecting position sizing/duration.

themes.theme: Private Credit Stress & Credit Cycle Signals

insights.claim: Gammon contends the roughly $3 trillion private credit market being smaller than the ~$60 trillion 2008 mortgage-backed securities market doesn't make current stress a 'nothing burger,' because the monetary system runs on confidence and counterparty-risk perception, not absolute market size — a shock to perceived counterparty risk has similar contractionary effects regardless of the dollar figure.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Don't dismiss private credit risk by comparing notional size to 2008; watch credit-spread and redemption-gate signals instead of headline AUM.

insights.claim: BlackRock recently halted redemptions on another private credit fund, following earlier redemption/liquidity problems at Blue Owl and Blackstone roughly six months prior — Gammon reads this sequence as evidence the credit-cycle downturn that began about six months ago is continuing to worsen despite falling out of news headlines.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Track private credit redemption gates as a leading indicator of credit-cycle deterioration even during periods of reduced media coverage.

insights.claim: Gammon does not expect forced Treasury-market selling from ex-US financial stress because banks act as the marginal buyer/seller there, but he does expect continued asset sales pressure in commercial real estate and private credit as foreign holders need dollars.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Differentiates which US asset classes are vulnerable to foreign dollar-driven forced selling (CRE, private credit) versus relatively insulated (Treasuries).

insights.claim: Gammon treats SpaceX's valuation decline (from roughly $220-250 down to about $150, near or below its IPO price) together with OpenAI and Anthropic reportedly delaying planned IPOs to 2027 or later as concrete evidence that speculative risk appetite and private-market liquidity are drying up, placing the credit cycle at roughly 'inning five or six.'
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Private mega-cap valuation marks and IPO-timing decisions are usable as real-time gauges of late-cycle liquidity conditions, ahead of public-market confirmation.

themes.theme: Passive Flows, Boomer Demographics & Market Structure Risk

insights.claim: Citing Mike Green's 'passive bid' framework, Gammon argues US equities have benefited from a roughly two-decades-plus tailwind of steady 401(k)/index inflows tied to a working-age population and low unemployment; as baby boomers (holding a reported $90 trillion in net worth) fully retire and stop contributing, that passive bid could flip into a passive sell, a tipping point Gammon believes may not be far off.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A structural, multi-year headwind for passive index flows is a distinct risk from cyclical/valuation risk and could persist even after a normal correction resolves.

insights.claim: Gammon's mechanism for a boomer-driven correction: the reported $90 trillion in boomer net worth is largely unrealized paper gains marked at illiquid top-tick prices; if forced retirement-driven selling meets thin real buyer demand (e.g., a stock priced at $100 with genuine buying only emerging near $50), the effective realizable value of that wealth could roughly halve, which could trigger panic selling as retirees watch their nest eggs shrink.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Illustrates how a demographic-driven sell-off could become self-reinforcing (price discovery gap triggering panic selling), amplifying downside beyond fundamentals.

insights.claim: Gammon connects boomer-driven US selling to foreign holders of US assets (e.g., Japanese pension funds owning Mag 7 stocks): if those countries need dollars more urgently than they need Mag 7 equity exposure (per the dollar-liability thesis), forced foreign selling of US assets could compound a domestic passive-outflow-driven correction.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Two independent sell pressures (US demographic outflows + foreign dollar-need outflows) could coincide, raising the tail risk of a sharper-than-expected US equity drawdown.

insights.claim: Gammon puts the odds at 'over 50 but not 75' that SpaceX's valuation will, in hindsight, mark the top of the current speculative venture-capital era, saying the outcome depends heavily on whether the unemployment rate — which he ties directly to the passive equity bid — starts rising.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Frames the unemployment rate, not a specific valuation level, as the trigger variable to watch for a broader risk-asset regime change.

insights.claim: Gammon argues Treasuries should hold up even amid stress-driven selling elsewhere, since banks maintain a persistent bid when the 10-year yield offers a wide spread over nominal GDP growth, and Treasury pricing is driven more by growth/inflation expectations than by deficit levels.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the popular deficits-drive-yields narrative; suggests Treasuries as a relative safe haven even in a dollar-stress/private-credit-stress scenario.

themes.theme: MicroStrategy, Stretch Preferred & Bitcoin's Dollar-Liability Trap

insights.claim: Gammon frames MicroStrategy (Strategy) as an ironic case study for his entire dollar thesis: despite its explicitly anti-dollar, pro-Bitcoin corporate stance, its preferred-stock dividend obligations are dollar-denominated, forcing forced Bitcoin sales as BTC's price falls — placing it in the same dollar-liability trap as Japan, India, or South Korea, except, unlike Toyota, it has no exportable product to generate offsetting dollar inflows.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: Bitcoin-maximalist balance sheets with dollar-denominated debt/preferred liabilities are structurally exposed to the same forced-selling dynamic as EM currencies during dollar strength — a risk factor for any BTC-treasury company, not just Strategy.
insights.quote: the poster child for hating the dollar is micro strategy. You know, the dollar is going to crash out there buying Bitcoin, the future of money, the future of currency, you know, hodal hold.

insights.claim: As of this recording, Strategy had just announced it would need to start selling probably billions of dollars worth of Bitcoin, not just a small amount, in order to service its dollar-denominated obligations as BTC price declines.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A concrete, near-term forced-seller data point for Bitcoin markets to monitor for incremental downside pressure.

insights.claim: Gammon floats an unproven hypothesis that some private credit funds, desperate for yield to cover distribution obligations amid a wave of redemption requests roughly six months ago, may have quietly substituted MicroStrategy's 'Stretch' preferred stock (paying ~11% dividends, sometimes levered to ~20%) for genuine credit exposure inside their opaque portfolios; with Stretch trading around 15-20% below par, he expects more private credit and pension funds exposed to it to reveal problems within six months.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A specific, checkable hidden-leverage thesis: monitor private credit and pension fund disclosures for Stretch preferred exposure as a potential contagion vector.
insights.quote: if that's your Hail Mary, if your Hail Mary for one of these private credit funds is stretch and your Hail Mary is trading at 80 cents on the dollar

insights.claim: Gammon's highest-conviction personal trade of the past 9-12 months has been a pairs trade shorting homebuilder Lennar (LEN) while offsetting it with an equal-dollar long position in SPY, expressing a bearish housing view (rates up hurts affordability, rates down signals economic weakness) without taking directional market risk; he estimates the trade is up roughly 40-50%.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A concrete, actionable relative-value template: express a bearish single-sector view via a matched-notional pair trade rather than an outright short or index put.
insights.quote: I don't want to short anything outright. It's it's too risky.

insights.claim: Gammon's rationale for pairs trades over outright shorts is that viewers who buy S&P 500 puts to bet against an admittedly overvalued market repeatedly 'get wrecked' because the bubble persists far longer than expected (he notes it's 'been in a bubble for 20 years'), whereas pairing a bearish single-stock view against a long index hedge captures relative value instead of market-timing risk.
insights.speaker: George Gammon
insights.so_what: A risk-management lesson for retail investors who hold a correct bearish macro view but keep losing money trying to time outright shorts/puts.

insights.claim: New Harbor's John Loer pushes back on the specific Lennar/SPY pair trade, noting homebuilders have recently shown broad technical improvement, so a housing short isn't a 'layup,' and cautions that dogmatic, thesis-driven pair trades risk getting an investor 'stuck' when one or both legs move against the thesis.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: A live counter-signal: recent relative-strength improvement in homebuilders is a reason to reassess, not blindly follow, Gammon's short-housing pair trade.

insights.claim: John Loer notes that custodians including Charles Schwab and reportedly Fidelity have institutionally limited the margin capacity available for long/short strategies, which he interprets as a signal that long/short pair trading has become popular enough with retail/advisory clients to be a potential contrarian crowding risk.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Rising broker-level restrictions on long/short margin can be read as a crowding indicator for pair-trade strategies broadly, not just Gammon's specific trade.

themes.theme: New Harbor's Portfolio Positioning & Market-Top Signals

insights.claim: New Harbor Financial runs roughly a 50% equity weighting split about 30% US / 20% non-US, citing that non-US developed and emerging market stocks have outperformed the US over the past year as a reason investors should not treat the US as the only viable equity market despite its outsized media attention.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: A concrete allocation benchmark (30/20 US/non-US within a 50% equity sleeve) for investors reassessing home-country bias in an expensive US market.

insights.claim: A Yardeni Research chart cited by John Loer shows Wall Street consensus 5-year forward S&P 500 earnings growth estimates at 23% year-over-year as of June 18, an extreme outlier versus the ~40-year historical data set (back to 1985) comparable to the dot-com bubble peak, which New Harbor reads as a classic sign of 'ill-placed optimism' propping up current valuations.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Elevated consensus long-term earnings growth forecasts are a specific, trackable late-cycle valuation red flag distinct from simple P/E multiples.

insights.claim: A Bank of America '10 factors' market-top checklist shows 7 of 10 indicators currently flashing, a level John Loer says resembles conditions seen near prior major peaks (July 1990, the 2000 tech top, January 2022), though he cautions this signals 'ingredients are in place' rather than an imminent top.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: A composite market-top scorecard investors can track for incremental confirmation without treating it as a precise timing tool.
insights.quote: dance while the music is playing, but stand really close to the exits.

insights.claim: Adam Taggart and John Loer argue classic sentiment indicators like the University of Michigan consumer survey are less reliable now due to a shrinking, non-representative sample, growing partisan response bias, and the K-shaped economy splitting sentiment sharply between the top and bottom of the K, reducing their historical value as leading market indicators.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Discount headline consumer-sentiment readings as a standalone market-timing tool; the K-shaped split may be distorting the signal more than usual.

insights.claim: Beneath the AI/semiconductor-led mega-cap trade, New Harbor sees a bullish broadening rotation forming, with meaningful relative-strength gains in small caps, financials (they recently added a new regional bank sector position), healthcare, and biotech, while cautioning that the AI/semiconductor concentration propping up index returns remains fundamentally cyclical, not a permanent 'utility'-like earnings stream as some bulls now claim.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: A tactical opportunity set for active managers to rotate into under-owned sectors ahead of an eventual AI-trade cool-down, rather than staying concentrated in mega-cap AI names.

insights.claim: Citing a GMO chart, John Loer notes roughly 60-70% of years since 1900 fell within inflation-adjusted 'lost decade' stretches for a 60/40 stock-bond portfolio, driven by acute but relatively short bear markets (the ~24-month dot-com bear and the ~17-month 2007-09 bear), a reminder that markets have historically spent more time in drawdown than the relatively benign past ~25 years most current investors have experienced.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Investors over 50 anchored to the past 25 years of mostly-bull-market experience should not assume the next 25 years will look the same; the historical base rate favors more frequent, damaging bear markets.

insights.claim: New Harbor holds a 10% precious metals allocation (5% miners, 5% bullion split evenly between gold and silver), having trimmed 2.5 percentage points of gold bullion on recent technical breakdowns from an original 12.5% weighting, and plans to hold the remaining position through further volatility rather than capitulate near what they believe may be a bottom.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: A concrete portfolio benchmark (10% total, split miners/bullion and gold/silver) for investors sizing a precious metals allocation amid recent weakness.

insights.claim: Mike Preston's technical read on gold and silver: gold is down roughly 28-30% and silver down almost 50% from highs across three successive down-waves, both trading below their 50/200-day moving averages (gold's simple moving averages just registered a 'death cross'), but he believes this more likely represents a healthy pullback within an ongoing bull market than a 2011-style top, pending a decisive close back above the prior breakdown line (roughly $4,100-4,200 spot gold, ~$65 spot silver) to confirm technical repair.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Specific price levels (gold ~$4,100-4,200, silver ~$65) to watch as the confirmation trigger for re-entering or adding to precious metals positions.

insights.claim: GMO co-founder Jeremy Grantham recently gave a high-profile, bearish CNBC interview where he was heavily challenged by the anchors; John Loer reads the harsh, dismissive reception itself as a sentiment indicator suggesting markets may be closer to a top, since prominent bears getting publicly ridiculed for being 'early' is a recurring pattern before major drawdowns.
insights.speaker: John Loer, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Media treatment of prominent bears can itself function as a contrarian sentiment gauge for approaching market tops.
insights.quote: missing out on the crazy party, but maintaining your capital and having that dry capital to deploy after the correction, he says, way more than makes up for chasing the the mania.

insights.claim: Mike Preston highlights a wave of pro-business election outcomes across Latin America — Colombia's new president, Mexico's first female president (described as pro-business), and Argentina's Javier Milei aligned closely with the Trump administration — and flags Brazil's late-2026 election as the next major swing country to watch, potentially unlocking investment opportunity both inside Brazil and for outside investors if it follows the same pattern (referencing prior guest Tavi Costa's bullish Brazil thesis).
insights.speaker: Mike Preston, New Harbor Financial
insights.so_what: Brazil's 2026 election is a specific, dated catalyst to monitor for a potential regional Latin American equity/currency re-rating if pro-business momentum continues.
